data.title: Plane-based Loop Correction for LiDAR Inertial SLAM
data.venue: International Conference on Indoor Positioning and Indoor Navigation

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1109/IPIN62893.2024.10786171?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1109/IPIN62893.2024.10786171, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract: LiDAR-based simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) exhibits excellent performance in indoor environments and is used to various aspects of robotic applications. Due to the inevitable odometry drift, map inconsistency always exists in mapping process. This issue is often addressed by loop correction, however, most loop correction method build pose to pose constraint based on appearance similarity, which is not able to detect map inconsistency with small overlap area.We proposed a plane-based loop detection method that is able to establish long-term association between plane feature and correct the drift without the need to go back to the same position. First, we identify map inconsistency between submap and global map. Then, we estimate relative transformation with revisited plane features. Then we utilize pose graph optimization (PGO) to correct the trajectory. Our proposed method has shown outstanding results compared with other state-of-the-art loop detection methods.

data.title: A Multi-Source Fusion Lightweight Tightly Coupled LiDAR-Inertial-GNSS SLAM for Unmanned Aerial Vehicle Detection
data.venue: 2024 3rd International Conference on Cloud Computing, Big Data Application and Software Engineering (CBASE)

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1109/CBASE64041.2024.10824651?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1109/CBASE64041.2024.10824651, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract: Autonomous outdoor inspections using unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs) are often repeated using RTK positioning technology combined with path-planning algorithms. However, positioning is often ineffective when RTK visibility is poor or strong electromagnetic interference exists near the UAV. Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR)-based simultaneous positioning and mapping (SLAM) technology combines RTK and INS information to form a multi-source fusion system that can provide robust positioning results for UAVs during RTK visibility interruptions. This paper proposes a lightweight, tightly coupled LiDAR-Inertial-GNSS SLAM framework, FLI3D, designed for indoor, outdoor low-altitude and high-altitude inspection scenarios. This framework constructs a Lidar-inertial system based on the state-of-the-art point-plane direct registration method. It introduces a lightweight Bow3D bag-of-words model as a loop closure detection solution to eliminate cumulative error. Additionally, the algorithm provides a GNSS signal fusion interface based on global factor graph optimization, allowing the system to integrate GNSS global observation information such as GPS and RTK signals, and employs a novel balanced fusion strategy to reduce overall computational resources for backend optimization. We conducted extensive experiments on datasets such as NCLT and MCD to validate the proposed method, and the results demonstrate that our system achieves high operational speed and robust performance.

data.title: Backpack LiDAR-Based SLAM With Multiple Ground Constraints for Multistory Indoor Mapping
data.venue: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1109/TGRS.2023.3332916?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1109/TGRS.2023.3332916, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract: High-quality 3-D point cloud maps are essential for precise indoor environment modeling. However, constructing such maps in multistory indoor environments is challenging due to the presence of narrow nonstructural spaces, such as staircases, corners, and corridors with similar textures. Simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) in these scenes is particularly difficult, as cumulative errors can lead to incorrect loop closures and drastic degradation in map quality. To address these challenges, this article proposed an SLAM method based on multiple ground constraints pose optimization (MGCPO), which uses a backpack light detection and ranging (LiDAR) system. The proposed method includes two novel modules. First, a regression analysis-based scenarios recognition (RASR) module provides a reference for the construction of ground constraints. Second, based on different scene detection results, the MGCPO module constrains the sensor pose using the floor plane to reduce localization errors and effectively decrease loop closure detection errors. Qualitative experiments demonstrate that our proposed method outperforms state-of-the-art methods in challenging scenarios. Quantitative experiments show that our method achieves an error rate of just 1.06% using only LiDAR sensors.

data.title: Unified Voxel-Centric LiDAR-Visual-Inertial SLAM for Large-Scale GNSS-Denied Environments
data.venue: Ubiquitous Positioning, Indoor Navigation, and Location Based Service

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1109/UPINLBS68186.2025.11468411?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1109/UPINLBS68186.2025.11468411, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract: Large-scale SLAM in GNSS-denied environments requires unified architectures integrating odometry estimation and loop closure detection while managing computational complexity. We present an adaptive voxel-centric SLAM framework employing a Hash-Octree structure for multi-modal information organization. The system extends single-camera voxel mapping to multi-camera configurations through multi-camera patch propagation. Each voxel maintains geometric plane parameters from LiDAR and multi-camera visual observations with temporal metadata. The Error-State Iterated Kalman Filter (ESIKF) tightly couples point-to-plane LiDAR constraints with multi-camera photometric residuals. Dual point cloud accumulators construct rotation-invariant descriptors in polar coordinates for efficient loop closure detection. Comprehensive evaluation demonstrates superior performance in kilometer-scale environments.

data.title: RID: LiDAR Range Image Descriptor for Fast and Efficient Loop Closure Detection in Indistinguishable Environments
data.venue: 2023 IEEE International Conference on Metaverse Computing, Networking and Applications (MetaCom)

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1109/MetaCom57706.2023.00141?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1109/MetaCom57706.2023.00141, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract: Loop closure detection is a crucial aspect of simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) systems in robotics, as it helps to enhance the accuracy and consistency of robot localization by recognizing previously visited locations. LiDAR SLAM is a widely adopted approach for localization tasks due to its robustness to changes in illumination. However, the use of existing global descriptors for loop closure detection in indoor environments with narrow corridors and confined spaces is challenging due to limitations in representing the environment’s geometry. To address this issue, we propose a novel approach to loop closure detection using LiDAR Range Image Descriptors (RID) that can effectively capture the geometry of indistinguishable environments with low computation cost. Our proposed descriptor compresses 3D point clouds onto a 2D image plane while preserving distance information. In this paper, we demonstrate the efficacy of our approach through experiments in large-scale indoor and outdoor environments, showing that our descriptor outperforms existing methods in terms of computational efficiency in challenging, indistinguishable environments. Based on the experimental results, our proposed approach is suitable for low-power robots and can be implemented in real-time, making it a promising technique for loop closure detection in challenging environments.

data.title: Performance of Different SLAM Algorithms for Indoor and Outdoor Mapping Applications

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.3390/asi4040101?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.3390/asi4040101, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract: Indoor and outdoor mapping studies can be completed relatively quickly, depending on the developments in Mobile Mapping Systems. Especially in indoor environments where high accuracy GNSS positions cannot be used, mapping studies can be carried out with SLAM algorithms. Although there are many different SLAM algorithms in the literature, each can produce results with different accuracy according to the mapped environment. In this study, 3D maps were produced with LOAM, A-LOAM, and HDL Graph SLAM algorithms in different environments such as long corridors, staircases, and outdoor environments, and the accuracies of the maps produced with different algorithms were compared. For this purpose, a mobile mapping platform using Velodyne VLP-16 LIDAR sensor was developed, and the odometer drift, which causes loss of accuracy in the data collected, was minimized by loop closure and plane detection methods. As a result of the tests, it was determined that the results of the LOAM algorithm were not as accurate as those of the A-LOAM and HDL Graph SLAM algorithms. Both indoor and outdoor environments and the A-LOAM results’ accuracy were two times better than HDL Graph SLAM results.

data.title: π-LSAM: LiDAR Smoothing and Mapping With Planes
data.venue: IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1109/ICRA48506.2021.9561933?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1109/ICRA48506.2021.9561933, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract: This paper introduces a real-time dense planar LiDAR SLAM system, named π-LSAM, for the indoor environment. The widely used LiDAR odometry and mapping (LOAM) framework [1] does not include bundle adjustment (BA) and generates a low fidelity tracking pose. This paper seeks to overcome these drawbacks for the indoor environment. Specifically, we use the plane as the landmark, and introduce plane adjustment (PA) as our back-end to jointly optimize planes and keyframe poses. We present the π-factor to significantly reduce the computational complexity of PA. In addition, we introduce an efficient loop detection algorithm based on the RANSAC framework using planes. In the front-end, our algorithm performs global registration in real time. To achieve this performance, we maintain the local-to-global point-to-plane correspondences scan by scan, so that we only need a small local KD-tree to establish the data association between a LiDAR scan and the global planes, rather than a large global KD-tree used in previous works. With this local-to-global data association, our algorithm directly identifies planes in a LiDAR scan, and yields an accurate and globally consistent pose. Experimental results show that our algorithm significantly outperforms the state-of-the-art LOAM variant, LeGO-LOAM [2], and our algorithm achieves real time.

data.title: HTD: A Hybrid Triangle Descriptor Combining Geometry and Intensity Information for 3D Loop Detection
data.venue: IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Biomimetics

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1109/ROBIO64047.2024.10907353?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1109/ROBIO64047.2024.10907353, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract: Loop detection is essential for a simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) system. For LiDAR SLAM, most of existing methods only extract geometrically-based features to search loops, which may lead to false positives when dealing with geometrically similar scenes in different locations, such as corridors with similar layouts on different floors in indoor environments. In this work, we propose a novel global descriptor, hybrid triangle descriptor (HTD), for 3D loop detection. HTD extracts corner keypoints and plane keypoints based on the structural information and the distribution of point intensity on the plane, and then builds geometry triangle descriptors and intensity triangle descriptors based on these keypoints. The triangle descriptors are invariant to translation and rotation, and can provide precise 6- DoF transformation between two loop frames. Besides, we propose an adaptive voxelization strategy to better extract keypoints and employ a hybrid scoring method to enhance the robustness of HTD. In our experiments, we compare our method against other state-of-the-art methods on public outdoor and indoor datasets (i.e., KITTI and TIERS) and our self-collected indoor dataset collected by a solid-state LiDAR. The results show that our method outperforms other state-of-the-art methods on most scenarios, especially on indoor scenarios.

data.title: Glass Detection in Simultaneous Localization and Mapping of Mobile Robot Based on RGB-D Camera
data.venue: 2023 IEEE International Conference on Mechatronics and Automation (ICMA)

openAccessPdf.disclaimer: Notice: Paper or abstract available at https://api.unpaywall.org/v2/10.1109/ICMA57826.2023.10216171?email=<INSERT_YOUR_EMAIL> or https://doi.org/10.1109/ICMA57826.2023.10216171, which is subject to the license by the author or copyright owner provided with this content. Please go to the source to verify the license and copyright information for your use.

data.abstract: Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) is a critical technology for modern mobile robotics. Robots with sensors like monocular, binocular, RGB-D cameras, and LIDAR can build maps of unknown indoor environments through SLAM and locate themselves in those maps. However, the presence of glass surfaces, which is a common feature in indoor scenes, can pose a challenge to the accuracy of SLAM due to their high transparency and reflectivity, which make it difficult for sensors to detect them. We propose an approach for detecting glass panels using RGB-D sensors and integrating them into maps with high precision using ORBSLAM2 and semantic segmentation. By leveraging RGB-D cameras to obtain RGB images and depth maps, and utilizing ORBSLAM2 to acquire map points and camera pose information, our approach locates glass panels accurately. The pixel-level classification of semantic segmentation is obtained based on the RGB image, and the position of the large plane is obtained based on the depth image. The above results are fused to obtain a more complete map containing the glass surface. The resulting maps include glass surfaces calculated within ±3° of angle error and ±0.1m of distance error. The results verify the effectiveness of the system and provide a solid basis for future research in this area.
